item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i think one of my favorite parts of this is the "[DATA EXPUNGED]" footnote on the "use of minors in scientific research".

It just has an implication of something REALLY awful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I’ve read over it twice and there are still some questions I have story wise, but that’s nothing new for me.

Structurally, I feel like this sentence:

Despite experiencing intense discomfort upon waking, individuals targeted by SCP-3290 do not have any physical symptoms related to massive blood loss experienced in the dream.

Belongs at the end of the previous paragraph rather than starting its current one; I feel like it has more to do with the previous paragraph’s subject than it does the paragraph it’s part of now. Possibly the following lines could be tacked onto the following paragraph as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a lot more straightforward than it seems. There's literally no twist to what the SCP does. There's really only one "actual twist" and it's nothing to do with the anomaly itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure I get what's going on here…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yikes! Coming across this and 847 in the same is quite the coincidence.

I don't know how I feel about this honestly. It's a eye-opening upon first read. I wasn't expecting what I got and it grabbed my attention.

I'll upvote it though. I Googled what it means to dream about mosquitoes so I'm definitely interested in what the connection is.

I think I have an idea.

edit I missed the bit about the vampires. Now I have no idea what's going on but I'm even more curious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mmm. That's disappointing because, as Hippo asserts, it turns the SCP into an anomaly which simply punishes wrongdoing. From a characterization standpoint, it's much more interesting to have the leech anomaly be too vigorous in its crusade than to be perfect at its job - and because the topic being handled is child sexual abuse, which goes unnoticed and unpunished far too often, the article becomes, in a very, very perverse way, a sort of wish-fulfillment article (the wish being for child predators to face justice).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fair. I think trying to explain my thought process behind this would get rid of the ambiguities I purposefully tried to put here behind the article (there's only one I wanted to clear up below; also, might as well mention this now, but I have 0 interest in explaining any origin for this SCP to anyone cause that would ruin the point of this having no addena). So I will say that I'm glad I got you to think and reflect on the article after reading it. It's very high praise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Foundation responses to different 3290-Events will depend on different circumstances. These include but are not limited to:

  1. Local community standing of the individual affected
  2. Global knowledge of the individual affected

Combined with referring events to 'disinformation specialists' I have trouble reading this other than the Foundation covering for some perpetrators (covering things up is what the Foundation does, after all, and if it was standard practice to refer all these cases to law enforcement, it would presumably have been stated outright).

I don't think the ambiguities are a bad thing for the article as purely a writing exercise, but if you wanted to convey a clear and specific message I don't think it works. I'll reserve judgement for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The crime of being a vampire...
IS A DEBT THAT CAN ONLY BE PAID IN BLOOD!

real talk, +1 tho

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Replying to this although I can't be sure we're referring to the same thing (the implication that the Foundation is involved in covering up worldwide institutional child sexual abuse).

Not what kaktus was referring to (I think), but that wasn't my intention to insinuate. If more people feel the same way though, I'll try to explain, and if people still feel the same, then clearly I need to do some editing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Replying to this although I can't be sure we're referring to the same thing (the implication that the Foundation is involved in covering up worldwide institutional child sexual abuse).

I have trouble deciding whether this lands in 'disturbing and thought-provoking' or 'soapbox-y/ham-fisted (as the author said) social commentary' territory. I suspect most readers will end up somewhere along this spectrum.

Novoted for that reason, but the article did make me think, to its credit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As Decibelles mentioned above, that wasn't my takeaway. The more interesting implication to me is the insinuation that the Foundation is experiencing this anomaly as a result of their ongoing research involving anomalies that involve children — or use/exploit children. If that wasn't there, this would be a hard sell for me — as it would just be a skip that punishes wrongdoing (like the one recent skip that punishes copyright abuse). ETA: Which is fine, btw; those kind of skips are just a harder sell for me!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll accept your downvote to explain this. I don't want people to get the wrong impression because this article means a lot to me and I wrote every bit of it very deliberately without tipping my hand (much; I'll fully accept the fact that this skip can be seen by some has hamfisted or soapbox-y). It's not giving out false positives. The way Hippo described it to me during critique sums it up very accurately:

[21:00] <Hippo> yeah the 98% implies that if it's not 100% accurate whatever it's 'missing' is just because of translation issues
[21:00] <Hippo> like it's not condemning innnocent people it's just not getting the guilty to tell the story perfectly straight

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The effect by itself is fairly bare-bones and not terribly interesting to read - but the twist that the anomaly is giving out false positives is enough to save it from a downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll talk about this with you on IRC when you get back from work. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know how I feel about what's being implied here.

item.title: Author's Post
item.content:encoded:
Here's my second attempt at writing a really good short SCP, since I tend to write decently-sized ones.

Thanks to the following:

  • The Great Hippo for helping me give this a good amount of extra punch. Turning my right hook into a haymaker, so to speak.
  • Ihp for critique.
  • Lazar Lyusternik for critique. I didn't implement any of their suggestions but they did talk about a couple of things that inspired me to tune a couple of things here and there.
  • tretter for critique.
  • Taffeta for critique.

This skip's mainlist title was inspired by Xiu Xiu's Brian the Vampire.

Music Listened To While Writing: Xiu Xiu's Fabulous Muscles.
